content.full_text:
Airport                                823-0744
Building                                823-4240
City Clerk                              823-4242
City Manager                            823-4299
Community Development  823-4244
Finance/Personnel                       823-4247
Fire                                    823-4265
Police                                  823-4237
Public Works                            823-4250
1225 Lincoln Way
Auburn, California 95603
FAX (916) 885-5508


October 9, 1991


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am City Attorney for the City of Auburn and recently spoke with
Mark Morodomi concerning a conflict of interest question. Mr.
Morodomi suggested that I could write and request advice more
formally. As set forth below, my question concerns whether
Auburn Councilmember Deborah Yue may participate in certain
upcoming council actions. In requesting your advice, I have set
forth some of the general background concerning the upcoming
council issues, the anticipated issues which will face the
council, and, finally, a brief summary of Ms. Yue's economic
interests.

BACKGROUND

Auburn has for a number of years had flooding and drainage
problems in the historic Old Town area. This problem is of major
concern for the City, as the Old Town area plays a vital role in
the commerce of the City.

The Old Town drainage basin, a tributary of Auburn Ravine, drains
the southeastern half of the City. The major run-off
contributing area lies between I-80 and the Southern Pacific
Railroad and extends south to Skyridge Drive and the Auburn
Recreation Park. The total area that drains into the Old Town
area is approximately 1 square mile or about 16 percent of the
total city area. In these drainage basins there are
approximately 600 residences in the Lincoln Way basin and 125 in
the Brewery Lane basin, representing a population of over 1,700.

There are two principal drainages that enter the Old Town
commercial/residential area. The larger is the Lincoln Way

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
October 9, 1991
Page 2

drainage from the north and the smaller is the Brewery Lane
drainage to the south. The confluence of the two drainages lies
beneath the boardwalk in front of the Old Town post office at the
intersection of Washington Street and Sacramento Street. The
combined flows are carried in a covered channel and concrete box
culvert under Commercial Street and discharges into an open
channel along I-80.

The problem is that the existing drainage system is grossly
inadequate. The century-old system cannot handle the run-off
from a storm of a ten-year magnitude. Current culvert drainage
systems in the Old Town area can only handle 150 cfs (Lincoln Way
basin) and 75 cfs (Brewery Lane basin). The estimated peak run-
off into the Lincoln Way and Brewery Lane culverts from a 1
percent or 100-year storm is 570 cfs and 170 cfs, respectively.
When this occurs, or even a storm of the 10-50 year magnitude,
the Old Town area is greatly affected. In 1986 the area flooded
due to a significant storm, and the damage to both private and
public property was such that the area was declared a disaster
area by the state. Businesses were closed for repairs, and the
loss to private property was substantial. The City suffered
major damage, causing street closures in the area. The damage to
the area disrupted commerce in the area for weeks. Both
businesses and the City suffered losses in hundreds of thousands
of dollars in terms of loss of trade, property and cost of
repairs.

The historic Old Town area is extremely important to the Auburn
community, acting as the primary entrance into Auburn. The area
contains many historic buildings built in the 1850s without the
benefits of today's engineering knowledge, and the loss of these
buildings would cause a rippling effect in the total community.
It is because of the key role of the Old Town area that the City
has placed a high priority on this drainage project.

FINANCIAL INTERESTS

Deborah Yue is married to Herbert Yue. Herbert Yue and Deborah
Yue reside at 215 Sacramento Street. This parcel is located
between Sacramento Street and Brewery Lane. The Yues own that
property in joint tenancy.

Herbert Yue and Harry Yue own property at 147 Brewery Lane, held
in joint tenancy between them. There is a small residential
structure on this property occupied by Harry Yue. Herbert Yue
and Deborah Yue receive no rental income from this property.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
October 9, 1991
Page 3

Herbert Yue is president and 25 percent shareholder of RHRH, Inc.
This is a small closely held family corporation.  RHRH, Inc.,
owns a parcel on Washington Street in the Old Town area, which
property is leased by Century 21 for real estate offices.
Herbert Yue receives a salary from RHRH, Inc.

RHRH, Inc., leases property at 289 Washington Street, in the Old
Town area, from Wilbur Yue.  The Shanghai Restaurant is operated
at this site.  The Shanghai Restaurant has sustained flood damage
in the past from major storms.

UPCOMING ACTIONS

Recently, the City Council approved a plan for both short-term
and long-term corrective measures regarding storm drainage in the
Old Town area.  Ms. Yue abstained from those actions, feeling
that it would be appropriate to do so until a formal opinion on
conflict of interest issues was rendered.  It is anticipated that
the following actions will be forthcoming and Ms. Yue would like
to know if it is appropriate for her to participate in these
issues.

     1.  Councilmembers also sit as members of the Auburn Urban
         Development Authority.  On October 28, 1991, the AUDA
         will consider budgeting funds for portions of the
         corrective measures pertaining to storm drainage in Old
         Town.

     2.  Later this year the City Council will consider a
         utility user fee ordinance, the purpose of which will
         be to help pay for corrective measures.

     3.  Early next year the Council will consider entering into
         contracts with municipal bond underwriters and bond
         counsel in connection with a possible bond issue to
         help fund corrective measures.

     4.  Next year the Council will also consider approving a
         capital improvement budget to fund a portion of the
         storm drainage project.  At roughly the same time AUDA
         will consider approval of its budget, including
         budgeting funds for the storm drainage project.

     5.  Finally, at some point after the above actions, the
         Council will consider approval of an engineering
         contract for the project, advertisement of bids for the
         work and consideration of bids for the project.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
October 9, 1991
Page 4

CONCLUSION

I realize that section 18702.3(a)(2) of the Regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission provides that the effect of a decision is material as to real property if the decision involves construction of, or improvement to, storm drainage facilities and the real property in which the official has an interest will receive new or substantially improved services. However, any improvement in the storm drainage facilities in the Old Town area will create a substantial benefit to the entire Old Town area commercial area and the City as well. Please provide whatever input you can on whether Councilmember Yue may participate in the upcoming issues outlined above. Most immediate, of course, is the upcoming issue of October 28, 1991, as outlined above. If at all possible, advice would be appreciated before that time.

If you need any further information or clarification of the information provided herein, please do not hesitate to contact me. Your assistance is greatly appreciated.

Yours very truly,

[signature]

CHARLES WACHOB
City Attorney

CW\ea

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Auburn Councilmember Deborah Yue may participate in upcoming city council actions regarding storm drainage improvements in the Old Town area, given her and her husband's financial interests in property that would be affected by the drainage project.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No verbatim QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. The letter discusses background, financial interests, and upcoming actions, then concludes with a request for FPPC input. Document references Section 18702.3(a)(2) of FPPC regulations. Some OCR artifacts present but content is legible.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether Auburn Councilmember Deborah Yue may participate in upcoming city council actions regarding storm drainage improvements in the Old Town area, given her and her husband's financial interests in property that would be affected by the drainage project.

embedding.summary: City Attorney of Auburn requests FPPC advice regarding whether Councilmember Deborah Yue can participate in upcoming council decisions on storm drainage projects in Old Town, given the Yue family's property holdings in the affected area and their business interests.
